pending_diagnosis: This case highlights the catastrophic consequences of a genetic metabolic crisis triggered by common environmental factors and medications. DIAGNOSIS: Acute Intermittent Porphyria (AIP) THE SMOKING GUN: When standard testing failed and the clinical picture became critically baffling, a targeted metabolic screen was ordered. Dramatically elevated Urine Porphobilinogen (PBG) and Delta-Aminolevulinic Acid (ALA) PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: Acute Intermittent Porphyria is caused by a partial deficiency in the enzyme Porphobilinogen Deaminase (PBGD), also known as Hydroxymethylbilane Synthase, the third enzyme in the heme biosynthesis pathway. 1. The Crisis: Stress, fasting, infection, or—critically in this patient—hormonal therapy (OCPs) and certain medications (Barbiturates, some benzodiazepines, Alcohol, or even the initial severe pain medication) induce Cytochrome P450 enzymes. 2. Heme Demand: Inducing P450 increases the demand for heme synthesis. 3. Toxin Accumulation: Because the PBGD enzyme is deficient, the pathway backs up, leading to the massive accumulation of neurotoxic intermediates: Porphobilinogen (PBG) and Delta-Aminolevulinic Acid (ALA). 4. Clinical Picture: ALA is structurally similar to the inhibitory neurotransmitter GABA, disrupting neural function throughout the CNS and PNS, causing the neuropathy (GBS mimic), psychosis, and fatal autonomic instability (tachycardia and hypertension). The abdominal pain is due to autonomic neuropathy of the visceral organs. 5. Treatment: Immediate administration of high-dose Hemin (or Human Hemin) to suppress hepatic ALA synthase activity and halt the production of the toxic precursors. Glucose loading is also vital, and strictly avoiding precipitating medications is mandatory. Ms. F.C. required prolonged mechanical ventilation and inpatient rehabilitation due to severe residual motor deficits.
